_description: Methodology template: Poverty Change Analysis (Two-Vintage Comparison). Pre-fills analysis section with change-detection method stack for tracking poverty trends across ACS vintages.

client.notes: Confirm the two ACS vintages before starting. Document whether tract boundaries changed between vintages — this requires crosswalk processing.

audience.role: Policy analyst, foundation program officer, or elected official
audience.what_they_are_deciding: Whether anti-poverty programs worked, where conditions worsened, and where to direct next-cycle resources.
audience.what_they_already_believe: COVID disrupted poverty trends in ways that complicate before/after comparisons.
audience.political_or_institutional_context: Findings may be used to argue for or against program funding. Frame as descriptive, not prescriptive, unless client explicitly wants advocacy framing.

engagement.hero_question: How did poverty, unemployment, housing cost burden, and income change between [Year1] and [Year2], and which census tracts show persistent or worsening need?
sensitive_findings_to_handle_carefully: COVID disruption on 2020-2022 data — flag explicitly, do not treat as clean trend
Tract boundary changes — crosswalked data should be flagged as modeled
Neighborhoods with demographic change may show poverty change confounded by displacement

known_spatial_issues: Tract boundaries may have changed between vintages — check Census TIGER for boundary changes
Merged or split tracts require crosswalk before change detection
Small tracts with high MOEs make change detection unreliable — flag in QA

primary_sources.name: ACS 5-Year Estimates — Vintage 1 (Earlier)
primary_sources.notes: Download for the earlier vintage. Store in data/raw/acs_[year1]/

primary_sources.name: ACS 5-Year Estimates — Vintage 2 (Later)
primary_sources.notes: Download for the later vintage. Store in data/raw/acs_[year2]/

primary_sources.name: Census Tract Relationship Files
primary_sources.notes: Required if tract boundaries changed between vintages. Use to crosswalk population-weighted estimates.

primary_sources.name: TIGER/Line Tracts — Both Vintages
primary_sources.notes: Download both vintages. Use later-vintage boundaries as the target geography for crosswalk.

known_data_quality_issues: ACS 5-year estimates are rolling averages — a 2019 5-year estimate covers 2015-2019. Comparing 2019 vs 2022 5-year estimates has overlapping years (2017-2019 appears in both). Change is not fully independent.
COVID-19 disrupted 2020 and 2021 ACS collection — 2020-2022 estimates should be used with caution for trend claims
Margin of error on change estimates: if both vintages have high MOEs, the change itself may be statistically insignificant
Displacement and gentrification can cause poverty rate to fall while absolute poor population stays constant or grows
institutional_areas_to_flag: Prisons and GQs distort poverty rates — the Census Bureau imputes poverty status for GQ residents differently
College tracts with large student populations show artificially high poverty rates due to $0 income reporting

analysis.dependent_variable_notes: Computed as poverty_rate_[year2] - poverty_rate_[year1]. Use derive_fields.py or compute_change_detection.py.

analysis_notes.change_detection: Use compute_change_detection.py or diverging choropleth in analyze_choropleth.py. Diverging color scale centered on 0 (no change). Red = increase in poverty rate. Blue = decrease. Natural breaks on absolute value of change, symmetric around 0.
analysis_notes.small_multiples: Four-panel map: poverty_rate, unemployment_rate, median_income, rent_burden — all for the later vintage. Use analyze_small_multiples.py. Shared spatial extent, individual color scales. Lets reader see which dimension drives change in their area.
analysis_notes.persistent_hotspots: Run Gi* on both vintages separately. Overlay hot spots from both vintages to identify 'persistent' hot spots (significant in both). These are the highest-need tracts — consistently elevated over time, not a COVID artifact.
analysis_notes.scqa_narrative: Use SCQA framing explicitly in the executive section. Situation = regional poverty context. Complication = change since [year1]. Question = which areas improved/worsened and why. Answer = persistent hotspots + specific change drivers.
analysis_notes.statistical_significance: Where possible, test whether the poverty rate change exceeds the combined MOE of both estimates. Flag tracts where change is within MOE range as 'no statistically significant change.'

report.pyramid_lead: Between [Year1] and [Year2], poverty rates increased in [N] of [Total] census tracts in [Study Area], with [X] tracts showing persistently elevated poverty across both time periods.

scqa.situation: [Study Area] had a regional poverty rate of [X]% in [Year1], distributed across [N] census tracts.
scqa.complication: By [Year2], poverty increased in [N] tracts and decreased in [M] tracts. However, [X] tracts remained persistently above [threshold]% poverty across both periods, suggesting structural rather than cyclical need.
scqa.question: Which communities have seen the most significant change — positive or negative — and which remain chronically high-need regardless of the trend direction?
scqa.answer: Persistent hot spots, concentrated in [geography], represent the highest-priority intervention targets. Poverty increased most sharply in [area characterization], while [other area] showed the strongest improvement. [Year1]-[Year2] comparisons are complicated by COVID disruption in 2020-2022 data.

additional_checks: Verify tract GEOID match rate between vintages — flag unmatched tracts
Flag tracts where abs(change) < combined_MOE as 'no significant change'
Flag GQ-dominated tracts (prison, college) before including in trend analysis
Check for boundary changes using Census relationship files

_methodology_caveats: ACS 5-year rolling overlap: ACS 5-year estimates are rolling averages. Comparing the 2015-2019 estimate to the 2018-2022 estimate means three years of data (2018-2019) appear in both. The estimates are not fully independent — this attenuates apparent change. For cleaner trend lines, compare non-overlapping windows (e.g., 2010-2014 vs 2015-2019).

COVID disruption on 2020-2022: ACS data collection was severely disrupted in 2020 (low response rates, changed methodology). The 2020 1-year ACS was not published. The 2018-2022 5-year estimates partially incorporate COVID-era data. Treat any vintage containing 2020-2022 as potentially noisy and flag in the executive summary.

Tract boundary changes: Census tracts are periodically redrawn, most substantially after each decennial census. Comparing 2019 (2010-boundary) to 2022 (2020-boundary) estimates requires a population-weighted crosswalk. Document crosswalk method in the technical appendix.

Displacement vs improvement: A declining poverty rate in a tract may reflect genuine economic improvement, or it may reflect displacement of low-income residents by rising rents. Cross-check with rent_burden_change and median_income_change. If rent burden rose while poverty fell, displacement is a plausible mechanism.

MOE on change estimates: The standard error of a change estimate is sqrt(MOE1² + MOE2²) / 1.645. Many tract-level change estimates will be statistically insignificant. Do not over-interpret small changes in low-population tracts.
